attributes.stem: A 42-year-old man presents with a one week history of progressive diplopia. He is otherwise well. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he is alert and oriented with vital signs within normal limits. Ophthalmic exam shows that he has left eyelid ptosis and the left pupil is turned down and out. The left pupil is 3mm larger than the right and does not react to light directly or consensually. The remainder of the cranial nerve and peripheral neurological examination is normal. His screening blood tests are unremarkable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has presented with evidence of an isolated complete oculomotor nerve palsy (cranial nerve III). The ocular findings arise due to the loss of action of structures innervated by the oculomotor nerve:

  • All extraocular muscles except superior oblique (trochlear nerve) and lateral rectus (abducens nerve) ➝ unmatched tonic action ➝ eye turned inferolaterally

  • Sphincter pupillae ➝ pupil dilation (mydriasis)

  • Levator palpebrae superioris ➝ eyelid sagging (ptosis)

The pupillary findings are highly significant in this case. Oculomotor palsy with pupillary involvement must raise suspicion of a compressive lesion, the most common aetiology of which is an aneurysm arising from the posterior communicating artery (PCommA), which is in close proximity to the oculomotor nerve just prior to the nerve’s entry into the cavernous sinus. Aneurysms are responsible for 20-30% of all isolated oculomotor palsies. If a PCommA aneurysm is detected in this patient, the presence of oculomotor palsy is an alarm feature that signals high risk of impending rupture and the patient should be considered for urgent treatment. As such, it has been written that “an awake patient with acute third nerve palsy with pupillary dilation should be assumed to have an aneurysm until proven otherwise”.

By contrast, oculomotor palsy with “pupillary sparing” (i.e. equal pupil size, intact light/accommodation reflex) is almost always related to ischaemic microvascular disease secondary to diabetes or hypertension. However, given that pupillary sparing may be seen in the early stages of compressive injury, aneurysm should be suspected regardless in patients at higher-risk age for aneurysm development (20 - 50 years old) or without diabetes, hypertension, or other vascular risk factors. A left parietal lobe tumour would not impact oculomotor function, however could potentially affect the visual pathway and cause a contralateral homonymous inferior quadrantanopia. Multiple sclerosis is unlikely in this patient by virtue of age and gender, and has been only rarely associated with isolated oculomotor palsy (via lesions in the midbrain affecting the oculomotor nucleus).
learningPoints: An awake patient with acute third nerve palsy with pupillary dilation should be assumed to have an aneurysm until proven otherwise
references: Heath Jeffery RC, Young B, Swann PG, Lueck CJ. Unequal pupils: Understanding the eye's aperture. Aust J Gen Pract. 2019;48(1-2):39-42. Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2019/january–february/unequal-pupils

Raza HK, Chen H, Chansysouphanthong T, Cui G. The aetiologies of the unilateral oculomotor nerve palsy: A review of the literature. Somatosens Mot Res. 2018;35(3-4):229-239. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30592440/
